title: Most cited scientist in the WORLD gives diet advice
text:
Wouldn't you love to hop in a Delorean
and go back to 1969 to see how people
stayed so slender?
I was there for it. My weekend job as a
teen was to caddy for one of the
greatest health influencers the world
has ever known.
>> I consider myself a scientific
nutritionist trying to get you to eat
the proper foods for beauty and for good
health. It's just that simple. Back
then, America's weight and health was in
the ballpark of other industrial
nations. But as the future came, a
disaster unfolded for America's health.
We should have predicted it because Doc
Brown and Marty McFly drove their
Delorean into the future to take a look.
Doc, where are we? According to my
calculations, this is THE YEAR 2015.
MARTY, the future. Great Scott. And it
only took Doc Brown a few minutes to
understand one of the problems.
>> All these people once got here in their
flying cars.
>> Uh, no. We You know what? We never
figured out flying cars. Actually, we
never did figure that out.
>> If you don't mind me asking, what the
hell are you guys been doing for 30
years? Oh, you know what? We invented
this thing called a cronut. It's like
part croissant and part donut, and they
put them together, and it's actually
quite delicious. Really good.
>> Oh man. and cronuts and Doritos. Doritos
were just boring corn chips before the
multibillion dollar artificial flavor
and color industries turned them into
sensations. But that can't explain it
all. Doritos are widely available in
countries like Japan, France, and
Denmark who are slender like we were in

1969 and France's is 11% today. So, Doc
Brown, is there a bigger factor than

>> I believe we may have inadvertently
traveled into an alternate
2015
where
human evolution has been stopped.
Apparently the the technological and
cultural achievements of this era
>> and that I believe gets closer to the
heart of the problem more important than

has gone in reverse. In 1969 we landed a

Armstrong Buzz Aldrin.
>> Everyone knew we led the world in
science. Times men of the year were
scientists. The American epidemiologist
DA Henderson led the effort to eradicate
smallox in the 60s funded by USAID.
He was honored with the Presidential
Medal of Freedom and that is what caused
me to pursue a career in science. But
tragically, while we led the world in
science in the 60s, we now lead the
world in science denial. Scientists are
no longer leading scientific agencies
like NASA. They're being fired and
countries around the world are
recruiting them. What does this have to
do with what happened to America's
health since the 60s, you ask? I think
trust and science is the second most
important indicator of a population's
health after poverty. For example,
here's what one of the most famous
doctors in America, Mark Heyman, who is
not a nutrition scientist, had to say
about nutrition science. 40 years of
lies. That doesn't sound like trust in

episode, you'll hear from the most cited
scientist in the world in any field who
is a nutrition scientist, and you can
judge for yourself whether he's lying.
Did you just have a big birthday?
>> Right. Yes. Yeah. the big 80.
>> I did. Yes. It was my goal to ride 80
miles on a bike for my birthday. I did
two days in a row of 85 miles.
>> Wow. Let's see if I can back up my very
bold claim that trust in science is the
leading indicator of a population's
health after poverty. First, here are
four countries whose dietary guidelines
have looked substantially the same for
50 years. The top three believe so
strongly in what Mark Heyman calls 40
years of lies. They teach it to their
children in schools. The bottom country,
America, leaves it up to everyone to
choose their own adventure. We can
choose from a plethora of mega
influencers with dueling diets. None of
them are nutrition scientists. All of
them have major side hustles to become
wealthy. Life expectancy in America is 5
to seven years less than it is in
countries that teach nutrition
guidelines from actual nutrition
scientists to children in their schools.
And those scientists rarely have side
hustles to get wealthy. More on that
later. Example number two,
Asian-Americans live 7 years longer than
white Americans in America, and they
have less than half the obesity rate.
And yet, white American Adventists near
Los Angeles outlive Asian-Americans by
roughly 3 years. Surveys consistently
place Asian-Americans as having much
higher trust in science than white or
black Americans. The only population I'm
aware of that scores higher are
Adventists because their church promotes

telling indicator of trust in science is
vaccination rate. It's consistently 95%
among Asian-Americans and much lower
among white and black populations,
especially in high mortality states in
the south where trust in science is
lowest. The only population I know with
even higher vaccination rates than
Asians are Adventists. This is a
longevity channel, so I'll be struck by
lightning
if I don't mention how profoundly
vaccinations affect longevity. I'm not
aware that vaccines have anything to do
with autism, but there is very
convincing evidence that they
significantly reduce our risk of
Alzheimer's. We even have a couple of
probable mechanisms, such as reducing

spend a ton of money on tech to improve

you can just walk to Walgreens and spend

pretty confident you've reduced your
risk of Alzheimer's. That's why I think
it's a national tragedy that the most
popular influencers who don't even know

never invite epidemiologists on their
channels claim epidemiology is weak or a

Imagine how many people are suffering
the tragedy of Alzheimer's because
they're not hearing from actual
epidemiologists.
I am a child of the 50s. Church bells

I need you to stay here while I travel

figure out where this all went wrong and

>> Oh, wow. All right.
Okay. I just set our comment section on

I'll make an episode on vaccines and

why do we believe in some branches of

Every environmental epidemiologist knows

They will lobby to cut your funding.
They may threaten to sue you. They will

shamelessly attack science for profit.
All epidemiologists have to defend

It's a tiny
super computer.

This must allow
uh astrophysicists
to triangulate complex

>> Yeah.
>> In real time.

Both of them have changed the world many

not everyone in tech is doing it for the

Epidemiologists are far more likely to

is directed at Walter. There is a
Norwegian doctor wealthy by marriage who

economists agree.
If we all simply follow the dietary
guidelines on meats, the world could
save more than $700 billion a year in

times as much as we spend on cancer

over the world had created dietary
guidelines, but nutrition had been their

thought to be the second biggest factor
in planetary destruction, including
deforesting half the earth. I gave a
TEDex talk about that. I have a lopsided

half of them, a little less than half,
to farmland. So on the left I have
forests and on the right I have
farmland. And somebody made the crack
yesterday that I represent deserts
really well. Well well where nothing
will grow. I don't think my look is
going to catch on but I get a big kick
out of Siri trying to grapple with face
recognition. Now
>> what happened to your face?
>> Did you It has created a global water
crisis. It adds greenhouse gases to the
atmosphere. it's a big poller, etc.
Gunhill made the primary focus of her
charitable foundation figuring out the
enormously complicated problem of what
can we eat that's healthy but also
doesn't poison our water or other
tragedies. What grabbed my attention
were the scientists that she recruited
to the cause. It reminds me of what
Steve Jobs told us so many times. Find

Rostrom to lead the earth science

greatest heroes for a very long time.
And she picked Walter for the nutrition
side and together they chose a team of
37 mostly university scientists from 16
countries with impeccable reputations
for Eat Lancet 1.0 in 2019. They worked
for three years on their first report
and then another three for eat Lancet
2.0, this time with 70 scientists.
Having worked in environmental
epidemiology, my first instinct was to
think, hm, which big industry will feel
so threatened by this, they'll launch a
huge PR campaign to fight it. They
always do. This report was one of the
most threatening to industries because
it's one of the most cited reports of
the last decade and influenced dietary
guidelines all around the world. Of
course, it was obviously beef because on
the nutrition side, every country's
dietary guideline recommends minimizing
red meat or eliminating it altogether.
Consumers in America are fleeing to
chicken. But beef's problems are way
worse on the environmental side than for
nutrition because beef towers over any
other food with regard to land use,
water use, greenhouse gases, and
poisoning our groundwater. You would
think that beef is in a terribly weak
position because science is not on their
side. So they did what every threatened
industry does. They hired a big PR firm.
Then they gathered whatever influencers
they could find that are willing to
defend the indefensible and launch a
coordinated campaign. You can do so many
things in a PR campaign scientists won't
do. One example is to turn it into a
culture war. Gunhild is wealthy, so just
claim the elites are telling us what we
can eat. After all, rage is the most
viral emotion. There is a nonprofit in
the Netherlands, changingarkets.org,
which made a report about how the
campaign worked. They received leaked
audio from a meeting the beef industry
held with influencers in Colorado where
they settled on messages and hashtags
like # yestome. One weakness in all
these campaigns is it's hard to find
credible scientists who are willing to
defend the indefensible even if they can
get a house in the Hamptons or a yacht
as a reward. In my view, the real
science that eat Lancet advanced was in
Earth science. We've had consistent
dietary guidelines around the world for
decades that are working for healthy
countries. What we haven't fully
understood in the modern era is which
foods are poisoning our water,
destroying our forests and oceans, and
heating the planet. How do we design a
healthy diet around foods that don't do
that? I could not imagine how they could
find a legitimate Earth scientist who
would defend beef. The Changing Markets
report listed the key influencers who
were defending beef in this campaign.
And to no one's surprise, most are
American. None are Earth or nutrition

to make things up. For example, here's
Brett Sh from Metabolic Mind. If you

would be to eliminate all animal
products um and eat a plant-based diet.
>> But he never showed what the Eat Lancet

slice of animal-based foods just like

world.
>> This dietary pattern is flexible. We

>> I would never um issue a document

Nutrition epidemiology in a nutshell,
